acceptance criteria: Notebook executes with nbclient or validate_ppg_course.py --chapter 19 --timeout 300.
Every major artifact is displayed or linked near explanatory prose.

Final sanity checks assert artifact existence, nonzero size, mirror reflection, angle-bisector direction agreement, circle-center reconstruction, focus construction, triangle concurrence, and relaxed nine-point conic residuals.
chapter goal: Show how Euclidean constructions and triangle theorems can be carried out projectively once the invisible circular points I and J are included.
computational checks: Mirror construction with complex circular points returns the ordinary Euclidean reflection with real residual below tolerance.
Laguerre bracket formula produces two real perpendicular angle-bisector directions matching classical unit-vector bisectors.
Tangents QI and QJ of a circle meet at the real center encoded in the circle matrix.
Circular-point tangents to an ellipse meet in foci matching sqrt(a^2-b^2), and sampled rays satisfy the reflection law.
The two confocal conics through a selected point satisfy constant sum/difference of distances to the foci.
Triangle medians, altitudes, angle bisectors, and Euler-line collinearity have small incidence residuals.
For several arbitrary H values, the six midpoint conic also contains the three cevian foot points within numerical tolerance.
concept inventory: Circular points I=(1,i,0), J=(1,-i,0) as invisible projective data that encode the Euclidean metric.
Mirror image of a point across a line from seven join/meet operations with I and J.
Laguerre angle formula and the bracket expression for the two angle bisectors of a pair of nonparallel lines.
Circle center as meet(QI, QJ), where Q is the conic matrix of a circle and the two tangents are complex conjugates.
Foci of a conic as intersections of the tangents drawn from I and J, including the real/complex pair split.
Conics with prescribed foci and a boundary point as the two confocal solutions selected by distance sum and distance difference.

Triangle theorem translations: medians via harmonic midpoint/Ceva, altitudes via orthogonality on the line at infinity, angle bisectors via quadrilateral-set conditions, and the Euler line via Desargues.
Hybrid thinking: the nine-point circle relaxes to a nine-point conic when the orthocenter hypothesis is replaced by an arbitrary point H.
implementation notes: Use only the assigned canonical notebook and assigned artifact subtree.
Keep source text as orientation only; prose, code, and diagrams are original.
Use complex homogeneous coordinates directly for circular-point algebra, but draw real Euclidean panels for learner inspection.

Use Matplotlib for static construction diagrams, Plotly for the confocal-conic parameter lab, SymPy for a small exact reflection identity, NetworkX for proof dependencies, and NumPy/SciPy-style linear algebra for checks.
Do not use utils.chapter_visuals or a generic render_chapter_visuals scaffold.

library routing table.fallback: direct Euclidean reflection formula for numeric comparison
library routing table.representation: complex homogeneous join/meet computation plus real construction diagram
library routing table.why this library: NumPy handles complex projective coordinates cleanly; Matplotlib gives a durable labeled construction; SymPy verifies a small exact reflection identity.

library routing table.representation: line-at-infinity bracket formula with two bisector rays
library routing table.why this library: The computation is algebraic and planar, so a static, equal-aspect diagram is the clearest inspection target.

library routing table.representation: conic matrix tangent computation and real center panel
library routing table.why this library: The actual tangents are complex; a real diagram plus matrix output avoids fake visible tangents.

library routing table.representation: ellipse, real foci, sampled reflected rays, and complex tangent equations
library routing table.why this library: The chapter's geometry is planar conic geometry with complex homogeneous data.

library routing table.fallback: static Matplotlib frame if Plotly is unavailable
library routing table.representation: standalone HTML slider over boundary points showing ellipse and hyperbola solutions
library routing table.why this library: Parameter variation is central: moving the boundary point changes both confocal conics while the foci stay fixed.

library routing table.representation: triangle dashboard and proof dependency graph
library routing table.why this library: Static diagrams expose concurrence and collinearity; NetworkX makes the proof dependencies inspectable rather than decorative.

library routing table.fallback: single H example if the residual table is enough
library routing table.representation: relaxed conic fit through six midpoint points plus cevian-foot residual table
library routing table.why this library: Fitting and evaluating a conic is linear algebra; the table records the invariant across several arbitrary H positions.

proof-visualization strategy: Use a dependency graph that links the invisible circular points to mirror construction, Laguerre angle bisectors, circle centers, foci via degenerate Brianchon, triangle concurrence via Ceva/quadrilateral-set conditions, the Euler line via Desargues, and the relaxed nine-point conic via Pascal/Hesse transfer.
risks: Circular-point tangents are complex, so the notebook must not draw them as ordinary real tangents.

The conic-from-foci projective dual construction is represented computationally by the equivalent confocal ellipse/hyperbola formulas; the notebook should state that this is the real Euclidean slice of the projective problem.
Notebook validation may fail if Plotly HTML display is attempted as a live renderer; the notebook should save HTML and display it through the course artifact helper.
source span read: Perspectives on Projective Geometry, Chapter 19, sections 19.1-19.7; printed pp. 349-374; PDF pp. 371-396.

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: See that the seven projective joins/meets land on the same reflected point, midpoint, and perpendicular line as the Euclidean mirror construction.
visual sequence.representation: real diagram driven by complex circular-point computation
visual sequence.validation: reflection residual, midpoint-on-line residual, perpendicular dot product

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Compare the two bracket-derived directions at infinity with the two ordinary angle bisectors.
visual sequence.representation: line pair with two bisectors and line-at-infinity formula annotation

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Notice that the complex tangents at I and J are invisible but their meet is the real circle center.
visual sequence.representation: circle, recovered center, and complex tangent coefficient panel

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Connect the real foci to intersections of conjugate circular-point tangents and to reflected rays.
visual sequence.representation: ellipse with foci, reflected rays, and tangent-line formulas
visual sequence.validation: focus-coordinate residual and sampled reflection-law residual

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Move the boundary point and watch the two confocal solutions through it: an ellipse and a hyperbola.
visual sequence.validation: constant distance sum/difference at sampled boundary point

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Read concurrence and collinearity as projective incidence statements with Euclidean meanings supplied by I and J.
visual sequence.representation: median, altitude, angle-bisector, and Euler-line panels
visual sequence.validation: centroid, orthocenter, incenter, and Euler-line residuals

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: See the nine-point circle theorem relax into a conic when H is arbitrary rather than the orthocenter.
visual sequence.representation: relaxed nine-point conic with residual table
visual sequence.validation: cevian-foot conic residuals for several H positions

visual sequence.learner_inspection_target: Track which earlier projective tools feed each Chapter 19 Euclidean result.
visual sequence.validation: graph node and edge counts plus named dependency coverage
